sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgical approaches often offer the most effective and efficient means for accomplishing cure. The primary goals are complete tumor removal and maximal preservation of function and cosmesis.

Principles:
- Surgical approaches are customized to account for individual factors, patient preference, tumor size, location, and risk features.
- Tissue rearrangement (e.g., flap reconstruction, extensive undermining) should not be undertaken until clear margins are identified.
- For tumors being considered for SLNB, delay reconstruction if not able to close primarily with minimal undermining.
- If invasion to parotid fascia is noted, superficial parotidectomy may be indicated.

Resectability Criteria:
Appropriate surgical margins should be determined case by case based on tumor and patient-specific factors. For low-risk lesions <2 cm, 4-mm clinical margins should result in complete removal in >95% of cases. For lesions >2 cm, 6-mm margins may be needed. For high-risk locations or features, margins of 4-9 mm may be required based on diameter.

Pre-operative Optimisation:
If clinical evaluation of incisional biopsy suggests that microstaging is inadequate, consider narrow margin excisional biopsy to obtain accurate measurement of thickness and anatomic level of invasion. Tissue rearrangement should not be undertaken until clear margins are identified.

cards.name: Curettage and Electrodesiccation (C&E)
cards.detail:
Approach: Fast and cost-effective technique for superficial lesions; does not allow histologic margin assessment.
Indication: Low-risk CSCC. Should not be used to treat areas with terminal hair growth. If subcutaneous layer is reached, surgical excision should generally be performed instead.
Key Complications: Lower cure rate than excision; risk of incomplete removal if tumor extends into subcutaneous tissue.
Operative Mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Mohs Micrographic Surgery or Excision with Peripheral and Deep En Face Margin Assessment (PDEMA)
cards.detail:
Approach: Surgical techniques allowing high-quality histologic visualization and interpretation of the entire marginal surface of excised tissue. Mohs is the most commonly used form of PDEMA. Must meet specific criteria (SCC-F).
Indication: Preferred for very-high-risk CSCC. Also a primary treatment option for low-risk, high-risk/very-high-risk, and laCSCC following multidisciplinary discussion.
Key Complications: Higher recurrence rates for recurrent tumors; risk of discrepancy between preoperative biopsy and Mohs findings.
Operative Mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Shave Removal
cards.detail:
Approach: Sharp removal by transverse bowl-shaped slicing to remove epidermal and dermal lesions without including fat; does not require suture closure.
Indication: Low-risk CSCC on trunk or extremities when tumor does not extend beyond the dermis.
Key Complications: Concern for inaccurate margin status assessment.
Operative Mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Standard Excision with Postoperative Margin Assessment
cards.detail:
Approach: Surgical excision with wider surgical margins (4-6 mm for low-risk lesions; wider margins for high-risk lesions) followed by postoperative pathologic evaluation of margins.
Indication: Low-risk, high-risk/very-high-risk, very-high-risk, and laCSCC when Mohs/PDEMA is not available. Also for positive margins after other modalities if re-excision is feasible.
Key Complications: Risk of positive margins; potential need for re-excision.
Operative Mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)
cards.detail:
Approach: Biopsy of the first draining lymph node basin to assess for subclinical nodal metastases.
Indication: Consider in cases of recurrent or multiple high-risk features for very-high-risk CSCC with normal exam of draining nodal basin (category 2B).
Key Complications: False-negative results; risk of postoperative recurrence/metastases despite completion lymphadenectomy.
Operative Mortality: Not specified.

cards.name: Regional Lymph Node Dissection
cards.detail:
Approach: Surgical removal of regional lymph nodes.
Indication: Preferred treatment for operable regional lymph node disease. Extent of surgery (e.g., parotidectomy, neck dissection) depends on number, location, and size of affected nodes.
Key Complications: Morbidity related to extent of dissection.
Operative Mortality: Not specified.
